summary: My name is Tay Ando, originally Teiichi, but I shortened it when I became a U.S. citizen. I was born in 1883 in Aichi Prefecture, Japan, and came to America around 1910. I worked hard as a laborer and eventually bought a 20-acre dairy farm in California around 1918, just as World War I ended. Despite hardships, I raised five children and saw them all go to college. I experienced many challenges, including evacuation during World War II, but I persevered.

I came from Aichi Prefecture in Japan, where I was born in 1883. Life there was simple; my father did a little farming, and schooling was hard to attend for poor families like mine. I left Aichi Prefecture around 1910 to seek a new life in America. Although I don’t remember much from my childhood, Aichi is where my journey began.

I came to the United States around 1910, arriving first in New York City. After about a year there, I moved to California to farm because I couldn’t get into school. I worked hard as a laborer, eventually buying land and raising a family. During World War II, I was forcibly evacuated and sent to camps but returned to continue farming. The U.S. government treated us fairly in some ways, though evacuation was hard.

I first arrived in New York City when I came to America around 1910. I hoped to attend school there but couldn’t, so I stayed only about a year. My second daughter was employed in New York during World War II and came back with us when we returned to California. New York was an early stop in my American journey but not where I settled.

After leaving New York, I came to California to farm, settling in Livingston near the Yamato Colony. I worked as a laborer and eventually bought land to operate a dairy farm. California was very hot and difficult at first, but I grew grapes, peaches, and almonds. During World War II, I was evacuated from California to camps but returned after the war to continue farming. My family and I have lived and worked here for many decades.

I settled in Livingston, California, near the Yamato Colony, when I came from New York. The colony was started around 1908, and when I arrived, there were grapes but also severe sandstorms. I worked in the Fresno area during grape season but eventually bought land near Livingston. The community was mostly individual farmers, and we had a church and social ties. It was a hard place to farm at first, but we persevered.

The Yamato Colony in Livingston was started around 1908 by Mr. Okue and others, mostly immigrants from Chiba Prefecture. When I came, the colony was still young, and we were mostly individual farmers, some brothers farming together. I bought a share from Mr. Okue and had 60 acres there, growing grapes, peaches, and almonds. The colony was a tight-knit Japanese farming community that worked hard to open up the land for agriculture.

After arriving in California, I worked in the Fresno area during the grape picking season because there was no work in Livingston at that time. I only worked there seasonally before buying my own land. Fresno was one of the places where Japanese laborers found work in agriculture. I had to repay money I borrowed in Japan, so I labored hard in Fresno before settling down.

Mr. Miyajima, who helped start the Yamato Colony, was from Chiba Prefecture, and immigrants from there were grouped together in one area. I met Mr. Miyajima a couple of times but don’t remember him well. The colony included people from Chiba, and that connection helped form the community in Livingston. Though I am from Aichi, the Chiba immigrants played a key role in the colony’s founding.

I bought my 20-acre dairy farm in 1918, which was just about the time World War I ended. The war marked a significant period in my early years in America. Though I was not directly involved in the war, it influenced the times and economy as I was establishing my farm. The post-war period was when I began to settle and build my life in California.

I was born in Nagano Prefecture but moved to Tokyo before school age. Though I am not from Aichi like Mr. Ando, I came to America and was shocked by the farming life here. Nagano was my birthplace, but my experiences in Tokyo and then America shaped my life. I had to learn farming from scratch after arriving in California.

I moved to Tokyo from Nagano Prefecture before I started school. Though I grew up in Tokyo, I did not attend school because it was hard for poor families. Later, I came to America and had to learn farming, which was very different from city life. Tokyo was part of my early life before I immigrated.

When I heard about the bombing of Pearl Harbor over the radio, I was shocked because I did not think relations between the U.S. and Japan were that bad. The attack changed everything, leading to our forced evacuation from our farm. It was a sad time because we had just started to earn income from our grapes. Pearl Harbor marked the beginning of a very difficult chapter for us.

The United States government treated us fairly in some ways during the war, though I did not think it was right to evacuate us from our homes. They organized the assembly and relocation centers where we were sent, like Merced Assembly Center and Amache in Colorado. While some Japanese were happy to be in camps, for us it was a hardship. The government also brought war veterans to nearby areas but they failed, while Japanese farmers persevered.

We were sent to the Amache relocation center in Colorado after staying at the Merced Assembly Center. Amache was much better than the Assembly Center, and I worked there as a sanitation man cleaning bathrooms and toilets. We stayed a little over a year before relocating to the eastern United States. Life in Amache was hard but better than the initial assembly center experience.

Before going to Amache, we were held at the Merced Assembly Center in California. The Assembly Center was much worse than the relocation center, and many people from Los Angeles barely making a living did not want to leave when it was ready to close. It was a difficult time, and we had to leave our farm behind. The Assembly Center was the first stop in our forced evacuation during World War II.

After leaving the camps, we relocated to Boston in the eastern United States. There, we did housework for a very nice family and made Caucasian friends whom we still associate with. Although it was hard to be away from home, Boston was a place where we found some stability during the war. My children were mostly grown and attending college during this time.

My younger son graduated from Stanford University after we returned to California following the war. It was a proud moment for our family, especially since all our children went to college despite the hardships we faced. Stanford represented the success and opportunities that came after years of hard work and sacrifice. Education was very important to us.

We heard about the German surrender while we had already returned to California in January 1945. It was a significant event during the war, marking the beginning of the end of global conflict. Though we were still recovering from our own hardships, the news gave us hope. The war in Europe ended before the war in the Pacific concluded.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: We heard about the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki after we returned to California. The bombings were devastating and led to Japan’s surrender. I thought it was natural that Japan lost the war because I did not support the Japanese military’s decisions. The bombings marked a tragic and decisive moment in history that affected us deeply.

The Japanese surrender came after the atomic bombings and was announced while we were back in California. I felt it was natural because I did not like the Japanese military and believed they should not have fought if they knew their strength. The surrender ended the war and allowed us to return fully to our lives and farms. It was a relief but also a time of reflection.

When we first arrived in Livingston, there were no single Japanese men around to hire, so we had to bring laborers from Stockton and Fresno. Some farmers in Stockton hired Japanese workers for packing grapes because they were good at it. Stockton was a source of Japanese labor during the early farming days in this area. It was part of the regional network of Japanese immigrant communities.

Some farmers in Sacramento hired Japanese workers for packing grapes because they were skilled at it. Japanese laborers were short-tempered, and over time, there were fewer Japanese workers around. Sacramento was one of the places where Japanese immigrants found work in agriculture. It was part of the broader agricultural labor system in California.

Many Japanese immigrants from Wakayama Prefecture came to this area, along with those from Hiroshima Prefecture. People from Wakayama were known to be less patient compared to those from Hiroshima. The community was diverse in origins, and these differences were noticeable. Wakayama immigrants contributed to the farming and social life here.

Immigrants from Hiroshima Prefecture were part of the Japanese community here and were known to be more patient than those from Wakayama. They worked hard alongside others to develop the land and farm. Hiroshima people were a significant part of the Yamato Colony and surrounding areas. Their patience helped in the tough farming conditions.

The government brought war veterans to Delhi, near Livingston, and gave them support to farm, but they failed and left. In contrast, the Japanese immigrants worked diligently and successfully cultivated the land. Delhi was part of the agricultural region influenced by these efforts. It showed how Japanese farmers opened up the land for farming despite challenges.
